code:
Hello, World!

This is my first webpage.

Visit Example

steps.title: The DOCTYPE
steps.explanation: This one line tells the browser: I'm an HTML5 document. Without it, the browser enters quirks mode — a broken compatibility mode from the 1990s where everything looks slightly wrong.
steps.why: Always write this as the very first line. No spaces before it, no exceptions.

steps.title: The <html> element
steps.explanation: Everything on the page lives inside <html>. It's the root container — the outermost box that holds your entire document. Notice the lang attribute tells the browser (and screen readers) what language the page is in.
steps.why: The lang attribute helps search engines and accessibility tools understand your content. Always include it.

steps.title: The <head> section
steps.explanation: The <head> is invisible to users — nothing here shows up on the page. It contains metadata: information about the page, like its title, character encoding, and linked stylesheets.

steps.title: The charset meta tag
steps.explanation: This tells the browser to use UTF-8 encoding — a system that supports every character in every language, including emoji. Without this, special characters can appear as gibberish.
steps.why: UTF-8 is the universal standard. Always include it as the first tag inside <head>.

steps.title: The <title> tag
steps.explanation: This text appears in the browser tab and in search results. It's not the same as the heading inside your page — it's metadata about your page.
steps.why: A good title improves SEO and helps users know what tab they're on when they have 20 tabs open.

steps.title: The <body> section
steps.explanation: Everything visible goes here. Text, images, buttons, links — all of it lives in <body>. This is where your actual page content lives.

steps.title: The <h1> heading
steps.explanation: <h1> is the most important heading on the page. Think of it as the title of a book chapter. It's large, bold, and tells both users and search engines what this page is about.
steps.why: Every page should have exactly one <h1>. Having more than one confuses search engines about what your page is about.

steps.title:
The

paragraph
steps.explanation: wraps a paragraph of text. Browsers automatically add some spacing above and below each paragraph, so your text doesn't look like a wall of words.

steps.title: The <a> link
steps.explanation: The anchor tag creates a clickable link. The href attribute tells the browser where to go. This is the foundation of the entire web — every hyperlink you've ever clicked was an <a> tag.
steps.why: href stands for Hypertext REFerence. It's the destination URL.
